title: Dog Eating Feces (Coprophagia): Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Discover why dogs eat poop (coprophagia), if it is an emergency, and how your vet uses photos to help diagnose the underlying cause of this behavior.
content_html:
TL;DR: Coprophagia, or eating feces, is a common canine behavior that is rarely an emergency but may require a vet visit to rule out parasites or nutritional deficiencies.

What is coprophagia and why is my dog eating feces?

Coprophagia is the technical term for a dog consuming feces, whether it is their own, that of another dog, or from a different species like cats or wildlife. While this behavior is quite distressing for pet owners, it is actually a relatively common behavior in dogs. It can stem from behavioral triggers like boredom or anxiety, or it may indicate an underlying medical issue such as nutrient deficiencies, malabsorption, or parasites.

Is it a medical emergency if my dog eats poop?

  • Urgency Level: Low. In most cases, eating feces is not a medical emergency.
  • The behavior can lead to the transmission of intestinal parasites or harmful bacteria like Salmonella and E. coli.
  • Monitor your dog for secondary symptoms such as vomiting, diarrhea, or lethargy.
  • Schedule a routine appointment with your veterinarian to rule out health problems rather than visiting the emergency room.

How can providing a photo of my dog's stool help with triage?

  • Providing a clear photo of the stool allows the medical team to assess consistency and color.
  • Visuals help identifying the presence of any visible parasites or undigested food.
  • This evidence helps determine if the behavior is linked to a digestive disorder or a behavioral habit that requires training interventions.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Coprophagia (feces consumption) in dogs warrants investigation into potential underlying causes. Fecal examination should be performed to check for parasites, especially hookworms and Giardia. Pathogenic bacteria, including Salmonella, Campylobacter (speciating C jejuni via PCR), and pathogenic Escherichia coli (identified via molecular techniques for pathogenicity genes) should also be considered. While the presence of undigested fat, fibers, or starch in feces may suggest malabsorption, these findings are unreliable.

Chapter: Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 404)

faqs.question: Is it normal for puppies to eat poop?
faqs.answer: Yes, many puppies go through a phase of coprophagia as they explore their environment with their mouths. Most outgrow it with age and consistent training.

faqs.question: Can my dog get sick from eating cat feces?
faqs.answer: Yes. Eating cat feces (sometimes called 'litter box snacking') can expose your dog to harmful bacteria and parasites, and the litter itself can cause intestinal irritation.

faqs.question: How can I stop this behavior at home?
faqs.answer: The most effective method is immediate cleanup of waste. You can also try adding vet-approved stool deterrents to their food or increasing mental and physical stimulation.
